notes: Official Origin product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
A dependent care FSA (DCFSA) is one of the most straightforward, high-impact tax moves available to working parents — and in 2026, it got meaningfully better. Since tax matters involve specialized expertise, I'll explain the concepts educationally. Per CFP Board standards, consult a qualified tax advisor or CPA for advice specific to your situation before making decisions.
How it works
A DCFSA lets you pay for qualifying daycare expenses with pre-tax dollars deducted from your paycheck. The money avoids federal income tax, and in most employer plans, it also avoids Social Security and Medicare (FICA) taxes. Many states follow federal rules, so you'd often dodge state income tax on those dollars too.
The 2026 limit jumped to $7,500 per household (for single filers and married couples filing jointly) — up from the long-stagnant $5,000 cap. That's a meaningful increase worth capturing.
How much could it save you?
Your actual savings depend on your combined tax rate (federal marginal rate + FICA + state). Here's a sense of the range on the full $7,500:
12% federal bracket (combined ~25%): roughly $1,850–$1,900 in annual savings
22% federal bracket (combined ~35%): roughly $2,600 in annual savings
24% federal bracket (combined ~37%): roughly $2,750 in annual savings
32% federal bracket (combined ~45%): roughly $3,350 in annual savings
The key insight: this isn't just an income tax break — the FICA savings alone add roughly $573 on a $7,500 contribution, which is money most people overlook.
What counts as eligible
Qualifying expenses include daycare centers, in-home nannies or babysitters, before/after-school care, and daytime summer day camps — as long as the care enables you (and your spouse, if married) to work. Overnight camps, K–12 tuition, and medical care do not qualify.
One important interaction to know
The DCFSA and the Child and Dependent Care Tax Credit (CDCTC) cover some of the same expenses, but you can't use the same dollars for both. The general strategy for most middle- and higher-income families is to max the FSA first, then claim the credit on any remaining eligible expenses above $7,500 — since the FSA exclusion tends to be more valuable per dollar at moderate-to-higher income levels.
Practical considerations
Elections are typically locked for the year (open enrollment), so estimate your daycare costs carefully — unused funds are generally forfeited
The limit applies per household, so if both spouses have employer plans, your combined contributions can't exceed $7,500
Your employer may set a lower internal limit (sometimes for nondiscrimination testing reasons), so confirm your actual plan cap
For a family with predictable, recurring daycare costs, maxing a DCFSA is often one of the highest-certainty tax moves of the year — no market risk, no complicated strategy, just straightforward savings.
Would it help to think through how the DCFSA interacts with the Child and Dependent Care Tax Credit based on your expected daycare costs this year?
Note: Calculations, potential savings, and eligibility determinations are estimations for illustrative purposes only. Tax laws change frequently. Consult a qualified tax professional before making decisions.
